title: Substrate Seed Spec v1 — What the Sieve Is Looking For
intent: Define the minimal set of structural claims that constitute a semantic governance substrate — what must be true for a system to qualify as one, what the sieve should promote, and what would invalidate the claim.

definitions.substrate: A layer beneath all application surfaces that governs how meaning is captured, transformed, and ratified — without imposing application logic.
definitions.sieve: A pure function that takes raw claims and produces a spine: what is structurally load-bearing, what is contested, what is deferred, and what is declared loss.
definitions.spine: The minimum set of claims that survive the sieve — structurally load-bearing, not just lexically relevant.
definitions.receipt: A hash-linked record proving that a specific function ran on a specific input and produced a specific output. Verifiable by anyone with the input and function source.
definitions.declared_loss: Claims explicitly dropped by the sieve with a reason. Loss is not silence — it is a first-class output.
definitions.human_gate: A structural enforcement point where ratification requires human provenance. Not a policy — an architectural invariant.
definitions.ore: Raw, uninterpreted material with provenance. Unshaped but witnessed. The substrate's input.
definitions.lore: Accumulated spine across multiple sieve passes. The narrative that survives compression.

context.problem: AI systems produce outputs with no chain of custody. Sessions die, context is lost, decisions are unattributable, and meaning cannot be verified independently of the actor that produced it.
context.mission: Prove that semantic transformations can be purely functional, hash-receipted, and independently verifiable — such that the substrate's trustworthiness derives from math, not from trust in any actor.
context.scope: The substrate itself — not any application built on top of it. Applications are projections from the substrate. The substrate is the primitive layer.
context.origin: Emerged from 326 Claude Code sessions (Feb-Mar 2026) working across multiple AI surfaces with no persistent thread. The substrate is the answer to what was missing.

transform.input_shape: Raw ore blobs: session files, conversation turns, terminal output, file changes — any timestamped, provenanced material.
transform.sieve_rule: Promote claims that are structurally load-bearing for the substrate's own development. A claim is load-bearing if: (1) it constrains what the substrate can be, (2) it defines a primitive or its relationship to other primitives, (3) it identifies a failure mode or invalidation condition, or (4) it resolves a contested design decision. Graph relevance score >= 0.1 required before keyword matching.
transform.output_shape: Spine: promoted claims with receipts. Contested: claims in structural tension. Deferred: real but lacking confidence signals. Declared loss: explicitly dropped with reason.

outcome.output_success: Sieve produces a receipted spine where promoted claims are verifiably structural — not just topically relevant. Declared loss accounts for everything dropped.
outcome.outcome_success: A cold agent reading only the spine can reconstruct the substrate's architecture, primitives, constraints, and failure modes without access to the original ore.

constraints: The sieve must be a pure function. No LLM calls in the promotion path.
Every sieve run must produce a receipt: input_hash, sieve_hash, output_hash, prev_receipt_hash.
Declared loss is mandatory — a sieve run with no declared loss is invalid.
Human gate is an architectural invariant, not a configuration option.
Graph relevance must be computed before keyword matching — graph score gates entry to the sieve.
The substrate must be substrate-agnostic: same primitives work on file systems, blockchains, or biological signal streams.
No primitive account is created until the upstream primitive produces a verified output artifact.
The receipt chain must be independently verifiable without running any LLM.

governance.ratifier_constraint: At least one human ratifies canonical promotion. Ben Fenton is the human gate for v1.
invalidation_conditions: The sieve is found to produce different outputs for identical inputs (non-determinism detected).
A receipt is found to be fabricated (hash mismatch on independent verification).
A primitive is built before its upstream gate is open (process violation).
The human gate is bypassed for any promoted claim.
Declared loss is absent from any sieve run.
governance.note: This spec is proposed, not ratified. It becomes canonical when Ben runs the sieve against it and the output independently verifies.

gate_sequence: ore captured + ledger verified → sieve account
sieve produces verified spine → graph account
graph produces verified graph.v1 → type account
type produces verified frame.v1 → check account

evaluation.output_success: Seed spec validates against spec_card_v1_0_1 required fields. Sieve run against this spec produces promoted claims that satisfy the sieve_rule definition above.
evaluation.outcome_success: The promoted spine from running the sieve against this spec contains at least one claim from each primitive category. Declared loss is non-empty. Receipt is independently verifiable.

failure_modes: Spec is too broad — everything promotes, nothing declares loss.
Spec is too narrow — real structural claims are filtered out.
Graph relevance threshold too high — misses semantically adjacent claims.
Graph relevance threshold too low — admits noise.
Sieve_rule is ambiguous — different runs produce different promoted sets.
Human gate becomes a bottleneck rather than a structural invariant.
Primitives built out of order — upstream gate bypassed.

degrade_policy.rule: A cold agent must be able to re-run the sieve from this spec alone. The gate sequence and invalidation conditions must survive any compression.

conformance.note: Proposed by Shadow (2026-04-09). Awaiting Ben's ratification after first sieve run against this spec.
